title: Deployments, rolling updates, and Services
description: Guided exercise: create a Deployment, observe its ReplicaSet, perform a rolling update, execute a rollback, and expose the application with a ClusterIP Service.

intro.text:
## Welcome to Exercise A

In this guided exercise you will work with the three fundamental building blocks of every Kubernetes production workload:

- A **Deployment** that manages your application replicas
- A **ReplicaSet** automatically created and managed by the Deployment
- A **Service** that provides a stable network endpoint in front of your Pods

Every step explains not just *what* to run but *why* it works that way. Read each explanation before running the command.

**Environment:** a single-node Kubernetes cluster is already running. `kubectl` is configured and ready.

steps.title: Step 1 — Create a Deployment
steps.text:
## Step 1 — Create a Deployment

A Deployment is the standard way to run a stateless application in Kubernetes. It manages a ReplicaSet, which in turn manages the Pods.

Create the manifest file:

steps.title: Step 2 — Observe the controller hierarchy
steps.text:
## Step 2 — Observe the controller hierarchy

When you applied the Deployment, Kubernetes automatically created a ReplicaSet, which then created the Pods. Let's verify the full hierarchy.

steps.title: Step 3 — Verify self-healing
steps.text:
## Step 3 — Verify self-healing

One of the key guarantees of a ReplicaSet is self-healing: if a Pod is deleted, the ReplicaSet immediately creates a replacement. Let's test this.

steps.title: Step 4 — Perform a rolling update
steps.text:
## Step 4 — Perform a rolling update

A rolling update replaces old Pods gradually, ensuring the application remains available throughout the transition. We will update the container image from nginx:1.14 to nginx:1.25.

steps.title: Step 5 — Inspect the rollout history
steps.text:
## Step 5 — Inspect the rollout history

Every Deployment update is recorded as a numbered revision. This history makes it possible to roll back to any previous state.

steps.title: Step 6 — Execute a rollback
steps.text:
## Step 6 — Execute a rollback

Imagine the new version of the application is causing errors. We want to revert to the previous revision. Kubernetes performs the rollback as another rolling update — availability is maintained throughout.

steps.title: Step 7 — Create a ClusterIP Service
steps.text:
## Step 7 — Create a ClusterIP Service

Pods have ephemeral IP addresses that change every time a Pod is replaced. A Service provides a stable virtual IP and DNS name in front of the Pods. ClusterIP makes the Service reachable only from within the cluster.

steps.title: Step 8 — Verify internal connectivity
steps.text:
## Step 8 — Verify internal connectivity

Verify that the Service correctly routes traffic to the Pods using a temporary debug Pod.

finish.text:
## Exercise A complete

You have:
- Created a Deployment and observed the Deployment → ReplicaSet → Pod hierarchy
- Verified self-healing: the ReplicaSet replaced a deleted Pod automatically
- Performed a rolling update with zero downtime
- Inspected the revision history and executed a rollback
- Created a ClusterIP Service and verified internal connectivity via DNS

These are the core operations you will perform on every Kubernetes cluster. Proceed to **Exercise B** when ready.
